01 / 14

Classical vs Modern NLP
on Biomedical Text

TF-IDF + Logistic Regression vs BioBERT

Dataset: ADE Corpus V2
Task: Binary Sentence Classification
23,516 sentences

Steven Cheun

5/13/26

02 / 14

Research question

How well do sparse word frequency representations compare to contextual embeddings for classifying biomedical adverse drug effect sentences?

03 / 14

Dataset — ADE Corpus V2

Source

MEDLINE case reports — Gurulingappa et al. (2012)

Total sentences

23,516

Label definition

Label 1Drug + adverse effect + causal link in one sentence
Label 0Everything else

Class distribution

Non-ADE71%
ADE29%

Split — seed 42, stratified

Train 70%  16,461    Val 15%  3,528    Test 15%  3,527

Language

English only, formal academic writing, pre-2010

04 / 14

EDA — Sentence length distribution

20.6
ADE average words per sentence
17.7
Non-ADE average words per sentence
ADE sentences are structurally longer. They must contain a drug, a causal verb, and an adverse effect within a single sentence.
05 / 14

EDA — Vocabulary by class

Shared vocabulary: patient, treatment, drug, report — no discriminative power. ADE-specific: induced, developed, following, severe — causal signal TF-IDF will learn.

06 / 14

Two approaches

Classical — TF-IDF + Logistic Regression

Sparse 20,000 dimensional vectors

ngram range (1,2) — unigrams and bigrams

No prior knowledge — learns from scratch

GridSearchCV 3-fold — C in [0.1, 1, 10]

Best params: C=10, max features=20,000

class weight=balanced — handles 1:2.4 imbalance

Modern — BioBERT

dmis-lab/biobert-base-cased-v1.2

Pretrained on PubMed 4.5B + PMC 13.5B words

Fine-tuned 3 epochs, lr=2e-5

110M parameters, 12 attention layers

Classification via [CLS] token then linear head

fp16 enabled — NVIDIA RTX 5070 GPU

07 / 14

Results — Test set (n=3,528)

ModelWeighted F1ADE class F1AccuracyErrors
MaxEnt + BoW (Gurulingappa 2012)0.70— *— *— *
TF-IDF + LR0.900.8490%349
BioBERT0.960.9396%145
* Weighted F1 only — ADE class F1, accuracy, and error count not reported in original paper
58%
fewer errors

Confusion matrix comparison

BioBERT

False positives: 224 to 96  |  False negatives: 125 to 49  |  BioBERT improved both error types simultaneously

09 / 14

TF-IDF — Learned token weights

Strongest ADE predictors

induced
+16.8
associated
+11.2
after
+9.1
developed
+7.7
following
+7.0
hepatotoxicity
+6.3

drug induced
-9.1
was
-7.1
improvement
-5.0
no
-4.7
successful
-4.3
the patient
-4.2

Note: "drug induced" negative despite semantic association — corpus artifact where phrase appears in meta-sentences not actual ADE events

10 / 14

Where each model failed

276
TF-IDF wrong, BioBERT right
Negation, meta-sentences, implicit causation. Requires sentence-level context that word frequency cannot capture.
72
BioBERT wrong, TF-IDF right
Overconfident on sentences discussing adverse effects academically without describing actual occurrence.
73
Both wrong
Cross-sentence implied causation. The genuine ceiling of sentence-level classification on this corpus.
12 / 14

BioBERT fine-tuning — validation performance across epochs

94.3%
Val F1 after epoch 1 alone
Strong start from PubMed pretraining. Val loss rises at epoch 3 while F1 plateaus — model converged. Best checkpoint correctly selected at epoch 2.
11 / 14

Error analysis — examples

TF-IDF wrong · BioBERT right

BioBERT wrong · TF-IDF right

Both wrong

13 / 14

Conclusion

BioBERT outperformed TF-IDF on every metric — +0.06 weighted F1, +0.09 ADE class F1, 58% fewer errors

TF-IDF learned real signal. Causal verbs "induced", "developed", "following" were correctly weighted. But it cannot distinguish context.

BioBERT's primary failure was overconfidence on meta-sentences discussing adverse effects without describing their actual occurrence.

73 sentences neither model solved. Cross-sentence implied causation remains an open problem in biomedical NLP.

14 / 14

References

Gurulingappa et al. (2012) — ADE Corpus

Development of a benchmark corpus to support the automatic extraction of drug-related adverse effects. JBI 45(5):885-892   doi:10.1016/j.jbi.2012.04.008

Lee et al. (2020) — BioBERT

BioBERT: a pre-trained biomedical language representation model for biomedical text mining. Bioinformatics 36(4):1234-1240   doi:10.1093/bioinformatics/btz682

L2D-Clinical (2025) — Benchmark comparison

BioBERT F1=0.911 on ADE Corpus V2   arxiv.org/abs/2604.13285

Dataset and Model

huggingface.co/datasets/ade-benchmark-corpus/ade_corpus_v2  |  huggingface.co/dmis-lab/biobert-base-cased-v1.2
